query: effect of tetrazolium chloride staining on necrotic versus viable myocardium

retrieved_docs.title: Myocardial Viability -- Evaluation -- Radionuclide Techniques
retrieved_docs.content: Technetium 99m Sestamibi SPECT: Tc-99m Sestamibi is a cation that diffuses across the cell membrane and then into the mitochondria, and positive uptake is suggestive of viable myocardial cells. The protocols used are rest-stress and rest protocol with nitrate enhancement to promote uptake. [16] Normal uptake is consistent with viable myocardium, moderate uptake is consistent with a mixture of infarct and viable tissue, and absent uptake is consistent with an infarct.

retrieved_docs.title: Anterior Myocardial Infarction -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Gross alterations become evident after 6 to 12 hours. Gross pathology reveals evidence of transmural infarction or myocardial necrosis in most cases of occlusion myocardial infarction (OMI). However, within hours of infarction, histo-chemically, the necrotic area appears pale with triphenyl tetrazolium chloride, indicating a lack of staining, whereas the uninfarcted area appears brick red. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Myocardial Viability -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The physiology of the ischemic cascade is fundamental for a stress test and myocardial viability assessment, as different imaging modalities utilize different stages of the cascade. The following imaging modalities are commonly used in the evaluation of myocardial viability.

retrieved_docs.title: Silent Myocardial Ischemia -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Healing continues and may be complete as early as 4 weeks or require 8 weeks or longer, depending on the extent of necrosis. Occasionally, larger infarcts may heal at the borders, leaving the central area with mummified myocytes for extended periods.

retrieved_docs.title: Stunned Myocardium (Archived) -- Evaluation -- Transthoracic Echocardiography (TTE)
retrieved_docs.content: Myocardial necrosis is associated with myocardial thinning; preserved end-diastolic wall thickness (EDWT) may provide a simple index of myocardial viability that can be assessed with a resting echocardiogram. EDWT less than or equal to 0.6 cm exclude myocardial viability. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Myocardial Viability -- Evaluation -- Radionuclide Techniques
retrieved_docs.content: The degree of uptake is proportional to myocardial blood flow. The strength of the emitted radiation correlates with the degree of uptake and, thus, the extent of viability. The infarcted myocardium does not take up tracers; hence, no radiation is emitted by this portion of the myocardium. The images show perfusion defects in that area. Radiotracer perfusion study thus aims to study the flow disparity component of the ischemic cascade, which is the early stage. The areas with normal perfusion are viable, but those with perfusion defects may be infarcted or hibernating. Metabolic tracers like 18F-FDG, as well as delayed imaging with Thallium-201, help differentiate this. The following imaging protocols are commonly used for viability assessment:

retrieved_docs.title: Myocardial Viability -- Evaluation -- Contrast-Enhanced Cardiac Magnetic Resonance (CMR)
retrieved_docs.content: The initial method of viability assessment using CMR is quantifying end-diastolic wall thickness (EDWT). A myocardial thickness of less than 6 mm is likely a 'scar' and less likely to improve after revascularization. One of the fundamental histologic changes in necrotic myocardium is extracellular volume expansion and death of myocytes. This change enables the high uptake and retention of high-density gadolinium-based contrast agents in the cell as well as extracellular space, termed late gadolinium enhancement (LGE) and forms the basis for a viability study with contrast-enhanced CMR. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Myocardial Viability -- Evaluation -- Radionuclide Techniques
retrieved_docs.content: Radiotracers used in the viability study are the analogs of our endogenous second messenger system and metabolites used in cell signaling and metabolism. Quantitative analysis of these radiotracers' uptake thus yields indirect evidence of intact cellular function and the cell membrane and subcellular organelles' integrity. These tracers have unstable nuclei and electrons, which emit characteristic radiation after being taken up by the myocardial cells, which are converted to photons and imaged by a camera system.

retrieved_docs.title: Myocardial Viability -- Evaluation -- Contrast-Enhanced Cardiac Magnetic Resonance (CMR)
retrieved_docs.content: Figure 1 shows a transmural LGE in the right coronary artery consistent with transmural infarction and lack of viability. Dobutamine stress CMR can be used to assess viability. A dysfunctional but viable myocardium will exhibit an increased systolic thickening of over 2 mm on low-dose dobutamine stress.

retrieved_docs.title: Myocardial Viability -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials
retrieved_docs.content: Extensive landmark studies assessing the mortality benefits of revascularization in a viable myocardium are not available, and studies are ongoing. Small- and moderate-sized studies showed no mortality benefits with revascularization and medical therapy versus medical therapy only in patients with viable myocardium. But these studies were statistically underpowered and had many confounding factors. In a sub-analysis of the STICH (Surgical Treatment for Ischemic Heart Failure) trial, 601 patients underwent a viability study before revascularization; about half received optimal medical therapy with CABG, and half received optimal medical therapy only. The arm with CABG and medical therapy had lower mortality, but it was statistically insignificant. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Myocardial Viability -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials
retrieved_docs.content: The STICH trial additionally showed that the increase in ejection fraction was more in patients with viable myocardium. Still, this increase was similar in both CABG with medical therapy and medical therapy-only groups. PARR-2 (FDG-PET assisted management of patients with severe left ventricular dysfunction and suspected coronary disease: a randomized, controlled trial) study showed PET viability imaging did not demonstrate survival benefits with revascularization compared to medical therapy. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Anterior Myocardial Infarction -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: The gross pathological, histochemical, light microscopic, and ultrastructural findings vary with coronary occlusion and ischemia onset.

retrieved_docs.title: Silent Myocardial Ischemia -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Characteristic histological features within the first 24 hours of insult include cardiomyocyte coagulation necrosis, neutrophilic infiltration, and interstitial edema and erythrocyte accumulation (see Image. Coagulative Necrosis in Myocytes During Silent Myocardial Infarction). Eosinophilic-appearing ischemic cardiomyocytes are evident with loss of the cross-striations and nuclei. Coagulative necrosis is established within the next 24 to 48 hours. After 3 to 5 days, myocyte nuclei and striations typically disappear in the infarct's central portion. After 5 to 7 days, macrophages and fibroblasts appear. At 1 week, neutrophil numbers start to decline, and granulation tissue forms with lymphocytic and plasma cell infiltration.

retrieved_docs.title: Rodenticide Toxicity -- Evaluation -- Troponin
retrieved_docs.content: Elevated troponin levels may signify cardiac myocyte damage caused by zinc and aluminum phosphide.

retrieved_docs.title: Myocardial Viability -- Evaluation -- Dobutamine Stress Echocardiography
retrieved_docs.content: Dobutamine stress echocardiography is used to assess the myocardial viability in patients with resting systolic dysfunction before revascularization. It is based on the principle that viable myocardium will undergo augmentation in contractility in response to beta-adrenergic stimulation, whereas non-viable myocardium will not. Dobutamine is infused at a rate of 2.5 to 10 micrograms/kg/min.

Recognizing that these diverse clinical presentations share the same myocardial and conduction‑system substrate and acute infarct morphology is essential for accurate diagnosis, timely therapeutic intervention, and preservation of both contractile function and electrical stability.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2399
retrieved_docs.content: Acute myocardial infarction, in its many clinical presentations—including ST‑segment elevation infarctions caused by occlusion of the left anterior descending artery, its septal or proximal branches, the intermediate artery, the mid‑portion of the right coronary artery, the posterior descending or posterior lateral branches of the right coronary artery, as well as non‑ST‑segment elevation infarctions, widespread infarctions, and those occurring during procedures or postoperatively—always involves injury to the myocardium. Regardless of the specific coronary vessel or segment that is blocked, the pathological finding site for each of these myocardial infarction subtypes is the myocardial tissue itself, underscoring that the heart muscle is the central organ affected across the full spectrum of acute infarct presentations. This unified understanding emphasizes that diagnostic evaluation, therapeutic interventions, and patient monitoring should focus on the extent and location of myocardial damage, irrespective of the particular coronary artery involved or the clinical context of the infarction.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 166
retrieved_docs.content: tissue in the pathophysiology and evaluation of AMI. Morphologically, acute infarct tissue links postoperative acute myocardial infarction and anterior non‑ST‑segment‑elevation myocardial infarction, indicating shared pathological changes across these clinical presentations. Acute subendocardial infarction is classified under “other specified forms of acute ischemic heart disease,” connecting it to a broader group of acute ischemic cardiac conditions. Together, these relationships underscore that coronary artery obstruction at diverse arterial segments is the primary mechanism driving myocardial damage, and that the common histopathology and diagnostic classification of the various AMI phenotypes are essential for accurate diagnosis, risk stratification, and targeted patient management.

retrieved_docs.content: further emphasizes the importance of recognizing valvular pathology when evaluating and managing cardiac patients. Collectively, the interconnections among acute myocardial infarction, various forms of heart muscle injury, coronary arterial thrombotic events, associated arrhythmias, and related systemic conditions illustrate the need for integrated diagnostic and therapeutic strategies in cardiovascular medicine.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 4 -- Community 2819
retrieved_docs.content: Acute myocardial infarction (احْتِشاءُ عَضَلِ القَلْبِ الحادّ) is closely related and may be considered synonymous with coronary artery occlusion (انسداد الشريان التاجي). It is also mapped to coronary artery embolism (انصمام الشريان التاجي), indicating that these terms describe similar pathological events in which a blockage of a coronary artery—whether by occlusion or embolic material—leads to sudden injury of the heart muscle. Recognizing the interchangeability of these concepts assists clinicians in diagnosing and managing the acute coronary syndrome that results from compromised coronary blood flow.

retrieved_docs.content: infarction during procedure, Postoperative acute myocardial infarction, and Subsequent anterior non‑ST segment elevation myocardial infarction. Recognizing that each of these specific infarct types shares the overarching classification of acute infarct highlights the importance of identifying the precise arterial territory or procedural circumstance involved, which guides diagnostic evaluation, therapeutic decision‑making, and prognostic assessment in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 1103
retrieved_docs.content: Acute myocardial infarction encompasses a spectrum of disorders that all involve injury to the myocardium. Specific forms include Acute Q‑wave infarction – widespread, Acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI), and Acute STEMI due to occlusion of distinct coronary branches such as the left coronary artery, the left anterior descending (LAD) artery, the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, the diagonal branch of the LAD, the intermediate artery, the marginal branch of the right coronary artery, the posterior descending branch of the right coronary artery, and the septal branch of the LAD. Additional variants are Acute myocardial infarction due to left coronary artery occlusion, Acute myocardial infarction due to circumflex branch occlusion, Acute myocardial infarction due to right coronary artery occlusion, Acute myocardial infarction occurring during a procedure, and Acute widespread myocardial infarction. Despite the differing anatomic sites of arterial blockage and electrocardiographic patterns (Q‑wave versus ST‑segment elevation), each of these entities shares the common finding site of the myocardium, underscoring that the primary pathological substrate in all these conditions is myocardial tissue damage. Recognizing the specific coronary artery involved helps guide reperfusion strategies and risk stratification, while the universal involvement of the myocardium highlights the need for prompt diagnosis and treatment to preserve cardiac function.
